1→//! This module implements the function inlining optimisation. This allows
     2→//! function calls to be inlined at the callsite, and replaced with the contents
     3→//! of the function which is being called.
     4→//!
     5→//! Function inlining is useful for two main reasons:
     6→//! - It removes the overhead of calling other functions and jumping around
     7→//!   execution too much
     8→//! - It removes the barrier of the function call between the code around the
     9→//!   call, and the code inside the called function.
    10→//!
    11→//! For example, the following Gleam code make heavy use of `use` sugar and higher
    12→//! order functions:
    13→//!
    14→//! ```gleam
    15→//! pub fn try_sum(list: List(Result(String, Nil)), sum: Int) -> Result(Int, Nil) {
    16→//!   use <- bool.guard(when: sum >= 1000, return: Ok(sum))
    17→//!   case list {
    18→//!     [] -> Ok(sum)
    19→//!     [first, ..rest] -> {
    20→//!       use number <- result.try(int.parse(first))
    21→//!       try_sum(rest, sum + number)
    22→//!     }
    23→//!   }
    24→//! }
    25→//! ```
    26→//!
    27→//! This can make the code easier to read, but it normally would have a performance
    28→//! cost. There are two called functions, and two implicit anonymous functions.
    29→//! This function is also not tail recursive, as it uses higher order functions
    30→//! inside its body.
    31→//!
    32→//! However, with function inlining, the above code can be optimised to:
    33→//!
    34→//! ```gleam
    35→//! pub fn try_sum(list: List(Result(String, Nil)), sum: Int) -> Result(Int, Nil) {
    36→//!   case sum >= 1000 {
    37→//!     True -> Ok(sum)
    38→//!     False -> case list {
    39→//!       [] -> Ok(sum)
    40→//!       [first, ..rest] -> {
    41→//!         case int.parse(first) {
    42→//!           Ok(number) -> try_sum(rest, sum + number)
    43→//!           Error(error) -> Error(error)
    44→//!         }
    45→//!       }
    46→//!     }
    47→//!   }
    48→//! }
    49→//! ```
    50→//!
    51→//! Which now has no extra function calls, and is tail recursive!
    52→//!
    53→//! The process of function inlining is quite simple really. It is implemented
    54→//! using an AST folder, which traverses each node of the AST, and potentially
    55→//! alters it as it goes.
    56→//!
    57→//! Every time we encounter a function call, we decide whether or not we can
    58→//! inline it. For now, the criteria for inlining is very simple, although a
    59→//! more complex heuristic-based approach will likely be implemented in the
    60→//! future. For now though, a function can be inlined if:
    61→//! It is a standard library function within the hardcoded list - which can be
    62→//! found in the `inline_function` function - or, it is an anonymous function.
    63→//!
    64→//! Inlining anonymous functions allows us to:
    65→//! - Remove calls to parameters of higher-order functions once those higher-
    66→//!   order functions have been inlined. For example, the following example using
    67→//!   `result.map`:
    68→//!   ```gleam
    69→//!   result.map(Ok(10), fn(x) { x + 1 })
    70→//!   ```
    71→//!
    72→//!   Without inlining of anonymous function would be turned into:
    73→//!   ```gleam
    74→//!   case Ok(10) {
    75→//!     Ok(value) -> Ok(fn(x) { x + 1 }(value))
    76→//!     Error(error) -> Error(error)
    77→//!   }
    78→//!   ```
    79→//!
    80→//!   However if we inline anonymous functions also, we remove every call, and
    81→//!   so it becomes:
    82→//!
    83→//!   ```gleam
    84→//!   case Ok(10) {
    85→//!     Ok(value) -> Ok(value + 1)
    86→//!     Error(error) -> Error(error)
    87→//!   }
    88→//!   ```
    89→//!
    90→//! - Remove calls to anonymous functions in pipelines. Sometimes, an anonymous
    91→//!   function is used in a pipeline, which can sometimes be the result of an
    92→//!   expanded function capture. For example:
    93→//!
    94→//!   ```gleam
    95→//!   "10" |> int.parse |> result.unwrap(0) |> fn(x) { x * x } |> something_else
    96→//!   ```
    97→//!
    98→//!   This can now be desugared to:
    99→//!   ```gleam
   100→//!   let _pipe1 = "10"
   101→//!   let _pipe2 = int.parse(_pipe1)
   102→//!   let _pipe3 = result.unwrap(_pipe2, 0)
   103→//!   let _pipe4 = _pipe3 * _pipe3
   104→//!   something_else(_pipe4)
   105→//!   ```
   106→//!
   107→//! See documentation of individual functions to explain better how the process
   108→//! works.
   109→//!
   110→
   111→use std::{collections::HashMap, sync::Arc};
   112→
   113→use ecow::EcoString;
   114→use itertools::Itertools;
   115→use vec1::Vec1;
   116→
   117→use crate::{
   118→    STDLIB_PACKAGE_NAME,
   119→    analyse::Inferred,
   120→    ast::{
   121→        self, ArgNames, Assert, Assignment, AssignmentKind, BitArrayOption, BitArraySegment,
   122→        CallArg, Definition, FunctionLiteralKind, PipelineAssignmentKind, Publicity, SrcSpan,
   123→        Statement, TypedArg, TypedAssert, TypedAssignment, TypedClause, TypedDefinition, TypedExpr,
   124→        TypedExprBitArraySegment, TypedFunction, TypedModule, TypedPattern,
   125→        TypedPipelineAssignment, TypedStatement, TypedUse, visit::Visit,
   126→    },
   127→    exhaustiveness::CompiledCase,
   128→    type_::{
   129→        self, Deprecation, ModuleInterface, ModuleValueConstructor, PRELUDE_MODULE_NAME,
   130→        PatternConstructor, Type, TypedCallArg, ValueConstructor, ValueConstructorVariant,
   131→        collapse_links,
   132→        error::VariableOrigin,
   133→        expression::{Implementations, Purity},
   134→    },
   135→};
   136→
   137→/// Perform function inlining across an entire module, applying it to each
   138→/// individual function.
   139→pub fn module(
   140→    mut module: TypedModule,
   141→    modules: &im::HashMap<EcoString, ModuleInterface>,
   142→) -> TypedModule {
   143→    let mut inliner = Inliner::new(modules);
   144→
   145→    module.definitions = module
   146→        .definitions
   147→        .into_iter()
   148→        .map(|definition| inliner.definition(definition))
   149→        .collect();
   150→    module
   151→}
   152→
   153→struct Inliner<'a> {
   154→    /// Importable modules, containing information about functions which can be
   155→    /// inlined
   156→    modules: &'a im::HashMap<EcoString, ModuleInterface>,
   157→    /// Any variables which can be inlined. This is used when inlining the body
   158→    /// of function calls. Let's look at an example inlinable function:
   159→    /// ```gleam
   160→    /// pub fn add(a, b) {
   161→    ///   a + b
   162→    /// }
   163→    /// ```
   164→    /// If it is called - `add(1, 2)` - it can be inlined to the following:
   165→    /// ```gleam
   166→    /// {
   167→    ///   let a = 1
   168→    ///   let b = 2
   169→    ///   a + b
   170→    /// }
   171→    /// ```
   172→    ///
   173→    /// However, this can be inlined further. Since `a` and `b` are only used
   174→    /// once each in the body, the whole expression can be reduced to `1 + 2`.
   175→    ///
   176→    /// In the above example, this variable would contain `{a: 1, b: 2}`,
   177→    /// indicating the names of the variables to be inlined, as well as the
   178→    /// values to replace them with.
   179→    inline_variables: HashMap<EcoString, TypedExpr>,
   180→}
   181→
   182→impl Inliner<'_> {
   183→    fn new(modules: &im::HashMap<EcoString, ModuleInterface>) -> Inliner<'_> {
   184→        Inliner {
   185→            modules,
   186→            inline_variables: HashMap::new(),
   187→        }
   188→    }
   189→
   190→    /// Perform inlining over a single definition. This only does anything for
   191→    /// function definitions as none of the other definitions can contain call
   192→    /// expressions to be inlined.
   193→    fn definition(&mut self, definition: TypedDefinition) -> TypedDefinition {
   194→        match definition {
   195→            Definition::Function(function_ast) => Definition::Function(self.function(function_ast)),
   196→            Definition::TypeAlias(_)
   197→            | Definition::CustomType(_)
   198→            | Definition::Import(_)
   199→            | Definition::ModuleConstant(_) => definition,
   200→        }
   201→    }
   202→
   203→    fn function(&mut self, mut function: TypedFunction) -> TypedFunction {
   204→        function.body = function.body.mapped(|statement| self.statement(statement));
   205→        function
   206→    }
   207→
   208→    fn statement(&mut self, statement: TypedStatement) -> TypedStatement {
   209→        match statement {
   210→            Statement::Expression(expression_ast) => {
   211→                Statement::Expression(self.expression(expression_ast))
   212→            }
   213→            Statement::Assignment(assignment_ast) => {
   214→                Statement::Assignment(Box::new(self.assignment(*assignment_ast)))
   215→            }
   216→            Statement::Use(use_ast) => Statement::Use(self.use_(use_ast)),
   217→            Statement::Assert(assert_ast) => Statement::Assert(self.assert(assert_ast)),
   218→        }
   219→    }
   220→
   221→    fn assert(&mut self, assert: TypedAssert) -> TypedAssert {
   222→        let Assert {
   223→            location,
   224→            value,
   225→            message,
   226→        } = assert;
   227→
   228→        Assert {
   229→            location,
   230→            value: self.expression(value),
   231→            message: message.map(|expression| self.expression(expression)),
   232→        }
   233→    }
   234→
   235→    fn use_(&mut self, mut use_: TypedUse) -> TypedUse {
   236→        use_.call = self.boxed_expression(use_.call);
   237→        use_
   238→    }
   239→
   240→    fn assignment(&mut self, assignment: TypedAssignment) -> TypedAssignment {
   241→        let Assignment {
   242→            location,
   243→            value,
   244→            pattern,
   245→            kind,
   246→            annotation,
   247→            compiled_case,
   248→        } = assignment;
   249→
   250→        Assignment {
   251→            location,
   252→            value: self.expression(value),
   253→            pattern,
   254→            kind: self.assignment_kind(kind),
   255→            annotation,
   256→            compiled_case,
   257→        }
   258→    }
   259→
   260→    fn assignment_kind(&mut self, kind: AssignmentKind<TypedExpr>) -> AssignmentKind<TypedExpr> {
   261→        match kind {
   262→            AssignmentKind::Let | AssignmentKind::Generated => kind,
   263→            AssignmentKind::Assert {
   264→                location,
   265→                assert_keyword_start,
   266→                message,
   267→            } => AssignmentKind::Assert {
   268→                location,
   269→                assert_keyword_start,
   270→                message: message.map(|expression| self.expression(expression)),
   271→            },
   272→        }
   273→    }
   274→
   275→    fn boxed_expression(&mut self, boxed: Box<TypedExpr>) -> Box<TypedExpr> {
   276→        Box::new(self.expression(*boxed))
   277→    }
   278→
   279→    fn expressions(&mut self, expressions: Vec<TypedExpr>) -> Vec<TypedExpr> {
   280→        expressions
   281→            .into_iter()
   282→            .map(|expression| self.expression(expression))
   283→            .collect()
   284→    }
   285→
   286→    /// Perform inlining over an expression. This function is recursive, as
   287→    /// expressions can be deeply nested. Most expressions just recursively
   288→    /// call this function on each of their component parts, but some have
   289→    /// special handling.
   290→    fn expression(&mut self, expression: TypedExpr) -> TypedExpr {
   291→        match expression {
   292→            TypedExpr::Int { .. }
   293→            | TypedExpr::Float { .. }
   294→            | TypedExpr::String { .. }
   295→            | TypedExpr::Fn { .. }
   296→            | TypedExpr::ModuleSelect { .. }
   297→            | TypedExpr::Invalid { .. } => expression,
   298→
   299→            TypedExpr::Var {
   300→                ref constructor,
   301→                ref name,
   302→                ..
   303→            } => match &constructor.variant {
   304→                // If this variable can be inlined, replace it with its value.
   305→                // See the `inline_variables` documentation for an explanation.
   306→                ValueConstructorVariant::LocalVariable { .. } => {
   307→                    // We remove the variable as inlined variables can only be
   308→                    // inlined once. `inline_variables` only contains variables
   309→                    // which we have already checked are possible to inline, as
   310→                    // we check for variables which are only used once when converting
   311→                    // to an `InlinableFunction`.
   312→                    match self.inline_variables.remove(name) {
   313→                        Some(inlined_expression) => inlined_expression,
   314→                        None => expression,
   315→                    }
   316→                }
   317→                ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleConstant { .. }
   318→                | ValueConstructorVariant::LocalConstant { .. }
   319→                | ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleFn { .. }
   320→                | ValueConstructorVariant::Record { .. } => expression,
   321→            },
   322→
   323→            TypedExpr::Block {
   324→                location,
   325→                statements,
   326→            } => TypedExpr::Block {
   327→                location,
   328→                statements: statements.mapped(|statement| self.statement(statement)),
   329→            },
   330→
   331→            TypedExpr::NegateBool { location, value } => TypedExpr::NegateBool {
   332→                location,
   333→                value: self.boxed_expression(value),
   334→            },
   335→
   336→            TypedExpr::NegateInt { location, value } => TypedExpr::NegateInt {
   337→                location,
   338→                value: self.boxed_expression(value),
   339→            },
   340→
   341→            TypedExpr::Pipeline {
   342→                location,
   343→                first_value,
   344→                assignments,
   345→                finally,
   346→                finally_kind,
   347→            } => self.pipeline(location, first_value, assignments, finally, finally_kind),
   348→
   349→            TypedExpr::List {
   350→                location,
   351→                type_,
   352→                elements,
   353→                tail,
   354→            } => TypedExpr::List {
   355→                location,
   356→                type_,
   357→                elements: self.expressions(elements),
   358→                tail: tail.map(|boxed_expression| self.boxed_expression(boxed_expression)),
   359→            },
   360→
   361→            TypedExpr::Call {
   362→                location,
   363→                type_,
   364→                fun,
   365→                arguments,
   366→            } => self.call(location, type_, fun, arguments),
   367→
   368→            TypedExpr::BinOp {
   369→                location,
   370→                type_,
   371→                name,
   372→                name_location,
   373→                left,
   374→                right,
   375→            } => TypedExpr::BinOp {
   376→                location,
   377→                type_,
   378→                name,
   379→                name_location,
   380→                left: self.boxed_expression(left),
   381→                right: self.boxed_expression(right),
   382→            },
   383→
   384→            TypedExpr::Case {
   385→                location,
   386→                type_,
   387→                subjects,
   388→                clauses,
   389→                compiled_case,
   390→            } => self.case(location, type_, subjects, clauses, compiled_case),
   391→
   392→            TypedExpr::RecordAccess {
   393→                location,
   394→                field_start,
   395→                type_,
   396→                label,
   397→                index,
   398→                record,
   399→            } => TypedExpr::RecordAccess {
   400→                location,
   401→                field_start,
   402→                type_,
   403→                label,
   404→                index,
   405→                record: self.boxed_expression(record),
   406→            },
   407
   408→            TypedExpr::Tuple {
   409→                location,
   410→                type_,
   411→                elements,
   412→            } => TypedExpr::Tuple {
   413→                location,
   414→                type_,
   415→                elements: self.expressions(elements),
   416→            },
   417→
   418→            TypedExpr::TupleIndex {
   419→                location,
   420→                type_,
   421→                index,
   422→                tuple,
   423→            } => TypedExpr::TupleIndex {
   424→                location,
   425→                type_,
   426→                index,
   427→                tuple: self.boxed_expression(tuple),
   428→            },
   429→
   430→            TypedExpr::Todo {
   431→                location,
   432→                message,
   433→                kind,
   434→                type_,
   435→            } => TypedExpr::Todo {
   436→                location,
   437→                message: message.map(|boxed_expression| self.boxed_expression(boxed_expression)),
   438→                kind,
   439→                type_,
   440→            },
   441→
   442→            TypedExpr::Panic {
   443→                location,
   444→                message,
   445→                type_,
   446→            } => TypedExpr::Panic {
   447→                location,
   448→                message: message.map(|boxed_expression| self.boxed_expression(boxed_expression)),
   449→                type_,
   450→            },
   451→
   452→            TypedExpr::Echo {
   453→                location,
   454→                type_,
   455→                expression,
   456→                message,
   457→            } => TypedExpr::Echo {
   458→                location,
   459→                expression: expression.map(|expression| self.boxed_expression(expression)),
   460→                message: message.map(|message| self.boxed_expression(message)),
   461→                type_,
   462→            },
   463→
   464→            TypedExpr::BitArray {
   465→                location,
   466→                type_,
   467→                segments,
   468→            } => self.bit_array(location, type_, segments),
   469→
   470→            TypedExpr::RecordUpdate {
   471→                location,
   472→                type_,
   473→                record_assignment,
   474→                constructor,
   475→                arguments,
   476→            } => TypedExpr::RecordUpdate {
   477→                location,
   478→                type_,
   479→                record_assignment: record_assignment
   480→                    .map(|assignment| Box::new(self.assignment(*assignment))),
   481→                constructor: self.boxed_expression(constructor),
   482→                arguments,
   483→            },
   484→        }
   485→    }
   486→
   487→    /// Where the magic happens. First, we check the left-hand side of the call
   488→    /// to see if it's something we can inline. If not, we continue to walk the
   489→    /// tree like all the other expressions do. If it can be inlined, we follow
   490→    /// a three-step process:
   491→    ///
   492→    /// - Inlining: Here, we replace the reference to the function with an
   493→    ///   anonymous function with the same contents. If the left-hand side is
   494→    ///   already an anonymous function, we skip this step.
   495→    ///
   496→    /// - Beta reduction: The call to the anonymous function it transformed into
   497→    ///   a block with assignments for each argument at the beginning
   498→    ///
   499→    /// - Optimisation: We then recursively optimise the block. This allows us
   500→    ///   to, for example, inline anonymous functions passed to higher-order
   501→    ///   functions.
   502→    ///
   503→    /// Here is an example of inlining `result.map`:
   504→    ///
   505→    /// Initial code:
   506→    /// ```gleam
   507→    /// let x = Ok(10)
   508→    /// result.map(x, fn(x) {
   509→    ///   let y = x + 4
   510→    ///   int.to_string(y)
   511→    /// })
   512→    /// ```
   513→    ///
   514→    /// After inlining:
   515→    /// ```gleam
   516→    /// let x = Ok(10)
   517→    /// fn(result, function) {
   518→    ///   case result {
   519→    ///     Ok(value) -> Ok(function(value))
   520→    ///     Error(error) -> Error(error)
   521→    ///   }
   522→    /// }(x, fn(x) {
   523→    ///   let y = x + 4
   524→    ///   int.to_string(y)
   525→    /// })
   526→    /// ```
   527→    ///
   528→    /// After beta reduction:
   529→    /// ```gleam
   530→    /// let x = Ok(10)
   531→    /// {
   532→    ///   let result = x
   533→    ///   let function = fn(x) {
   534→    ///     let y = x + 4
   535→    ///     int.to_string(y)
   536→    ///   }
   537→    ///   case result {
   538→    ///     Ok(value) -> Ok(function(value))
   539→    ///     Error(error) -> Error(error)
   540→    ///   }
   541→    /// }
   542→    /// ```
   543→    ///
   544→    /// And finally, after the final optimising pass, where this inlining process
   545→    /// is repeated:
   546→    /// ```gleam
   547→    /// let x = Ok(10)
   548→    /// case x {
   549→    ///   Ok(value) -> Ok({
   550→    ///     let y = x + 4
   551→    ///     int.to_string(y)
   552→    ///   })
   553→    ///   Error(error) -> Error(error)
   554→    /// }
   555→    /// ```
   556→    ///
   557→    fn call(
   558→        &mut self,
   559→        location: SrcSpan,
   560→        type_: Arc<Type>,
   561→        function: Box<TypedExpr>,
   562→        arguments: Vec<TypedCallArg>,
   563→    ) -> TypedExpr {
   564→        let arguments = arguments
   565→            .into_iter()
   566→            .map(
   567→                |TypedCallArg {
   568→                     label,
   569→                     location,
   570→                     value,
   571→                     implicit,
   572→                 }| TypedCallArg {
   573→                    label,
   574→                    location,
   575→                    value: self.expression(value),
   576→                    implicit,
   577→                },
   578→            )
   579→            .collect();
   580→
   581→        // First, we traverse the left-hand side of this call. If this is called
   582→        // inside another inlined function, this could potentially inline an
   583→        // argument, allowing further inlining.
   584→        let function = self.expression(*function);
   585→
   586→        // If the left-hand side is in a block for some reason, for example
   587→        // `{ fn(x) { x + 1 } }(10)`, we still want to be able to inline it.
   588→        let function = expand_block(function);
   589→
   590→        let function = match function {
   591→            TypedExpr::Var {
   592→                ref constructor,
   593→                ref name,
   594→                ..
   595→            } => match &constructor.variant {
   596→                ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleFn { module, .. } => {
   597→                    // If the function is in the list of inlinable functions in
   598→                    // the module it belongs to, we can inline it!
   599→                    if let Some(function) = self
   600→                        .modules
   601→                        .get(module)
   602→                        .and_then(|module| module.inline_functions.get(name))
   603→                    {
   604→                        // First, we do the actual inlining, by converting it to
   605→                        // an anonymous function.
   606→                        let (parameters, body) = function.to_anonymous_function();
   607→                        // Then, we perform beta reduction, inlining the call to
   608→                        // the anonymous function.
   609→                        return self.inline_anonymous_function_call(
   610→                            &parameters,
   611→                            arguments,
   612→                            body,
   613→                            &function.inlinable_parameters,
   614→                        );
   615→                    } else {
   616→                        function
   617→                    }
   618→                }
   619→                // We cannot inline local variables or constants, as we do not
   620→                // have enough information to inline them. Records are not actually
   621→                // function calls, so they also cannot be inlined.
   622→                ValueConstructorVariant::LocalVariable { .. }
   623→                | ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleConstant { .. }
   624→                | ValueConstructorVariant::LocalConstant { .. }
   625→                | ValueConstructorVariant::Record { .. } => function,
   626→            },
   627→            TypedExpr::ModuleSelect {
   628→                ref constructor,
   629→                label: ref name,
   630→                ref module_name,
   631→                ..
   632→            } => match constructor {
   633→                // We use the same logic here as for `TypedExpr::Var` above.
   634→                ModuleValueConstructor::Fn { .. } => {
   635→                    if let Some(function) = self
   636→                        .modules
   637→                        .get(module_name)
   638→                        .and_then(|module| module.inline_functions.get(name))
   639→                    {
   640→                        let (parameters, body) = function.to_anonymous_function();
   641→                        return self.inline_anonymous_function_call(
   642→                            &parameters,
   643→                            arguments,
   644→                            body,
   645→                            &function.inlinable_parameters,
   646→                        );
   647→                    } else {
   648→                        function
   649→                    }
   650→                }
   651→                ModuleValueConstructor::Record { .. } | ModuleValueConstructor::Constant { .. } => {
   652→                    function
   653→                }
   654→            },
   655→            // Direct calls to anonymous functions can always be inlined
   656→            TypedExpr::Fn {
   657→                arguments: parameters,
   658→                body,
   659→                ..
   660→            } => {
   661→                let inlinable_parameters = find_inlinable_parameters(&parameters, &body);
   662→                return self.inline_anonymous_function_call(
   663→                    &parameters,
   664→                    arguments,
   665→                    body,
   666→                    &inlinable_parameters,
   667→                );
   668→            }
   669→            TypedExpr::Int { .. }
   670→            | TypedExpr::Float { .. }
   671→            | TypedExpr::String { .. }
   672→            | TypedExpr::Block { .. }
   673→            | TypedExpr::Pipeline { .. }
   674→            | TypedExpr::List { .. }
   675→            | TypedExpr::Call { .. }
   676→            | TypedExpr::BinOp { .. }
   677→            | TypedExpr::Case { .. }
   678→            | TypedExpr::RecordAccess { .. }
   679→            | TypedExpr::Tuple { .. }
   680→            | TypedExpr::TupleIndex { .. }
   681→            | TypedExpr::Todo { .. }
   682→            | TypedExpr::Panic { .. }
   683→            | TypedExpr::Echo { .. }
   684→            | TypedExpr::BitArray { .. }
   685→            | TypedExpr::RecordUpdate { .. }
   686→            | TypedExpr::NegateBool { .. }
   687→            | TypedExpr::NegateInt { .. }
   688→            | TypedExpr::Invalid { .. } => function,
   689→        };
   690→
   691→        TypedExpr::Call {
   692→            location,
   693→            type_,
   694→            fun: Box::new(function),
   695→            arguments,
   696→        }
   697→    }
   698→
   699→    /// Turn a call to an anonymous function into a block with assignments.
   700→    fn inline_anonymous_function_call(
   701→        &mut self,
   702→        parameters: &[TypedArg],
   703→        arguments: Vec<TypedCallArg>,
   704→        body: Vec1<TypedStatement>,
   705→        inlinable_parameters: &[EcoString],
   706→    ) -> TypedExpr {
   707→        // Arguments to this call that can be inlined, and do not need an assignment.
   708→        let mut inline = HashMap::new();
   709→
   710→        // We start by collecting all the assignments for parameters which cannot
   711→        // be inlined.
   712→        let mut statements = parameters
   713→            .iter()
   714→            .zip(arguments)
   715→            .filter_map(|(parameter, argument)| {
   716→                let name = parameter.get_variable_name().cloned().unwrap_or("_".into());
   717→
   718→                // An argument can be inlined if it is only used once (stored in
   719→                // the `InlineFunction` structure), and it is pure. Sometime impure
   720→                // arguments can be inlined, but for simplicity we avoid inlining
   721→                // all impure arguments for now. This heuristic can be improved
   722→                // later.
   723→                if inlinable_parameters.contains(&name)
   724→                    && argument.value.is_pure_value_constructor()
   725→                {
   726→                    _ = inline.insert(name, argument.value);
   727→                    return None;
   728→                }
   729→
   730→                let type_ = argument.value.type_();
   731→
   732→                // Otherwise, we make an assignment which assigns the value of
   733→                // the argument to the correct parameter name.
   734→                Some(Statement::Assignment(Box::new(Assignment {
   735→                    location: BLANK_LOCATION,
   736→                    value: argument.value,
   737→                    pattern: TypedPattern::Variable {
   738→                        location: BLANK_LOCATION,
   739→                        name: name.clone(),
   740→                        type_: type_.clone(),
   741→                        origin: VariableOrigin::generated(),
   742→                    },
   743→                    kind: AssignmentKind::Generated,
   744→                    compiled_case: CompiledCase::simple_variable_assignment(name, type_),
   745→                    annotation: None,
   746→                })))
   747→            })
   748→            .collect_vec();
   749→
   750→        // If we are performing inlining within an already inlined function, there
   751→        // might be inlinable variables in the outer scope. However, these cannot be
   752→        // inlined inside a nested function, so they are saved and restored afterwards.
   753→        let inline_variables = std::mem::replace(&mut self.inline_variables, inline);
   754→
   755→        // Perform inlining on each of the statements in this function's body,
   756→        // potentially inlining parameters and function calls inside this function.
   757→        statements.extend(body.into_iter().map(|statement| self.statement(statement)));
   758→
   759→        self.inline_variables = inline_variables;
   760→
   761→        // We try to expand this block, so a function which is inlined as a
   762→        // single expression does not get wrapped unnecessarily
   763→        expand_block(TypedExpr::Block {
   764→            location: BLANK_LOCATION,
   765→            statements: statements
   766→                .try_into()
   767→                .expect("Type checking ensures there is at least one statement"),
   768→        })
   769→    }
   770→
   771→    fn pipeline(
   772→        &mut self,
   773→        location: SrcSpan,
   774→        first_value: TypedPipelineAssignment,
   775→        assignments: Vec<(TypedPipelineAssignment, PipelineAssignmentKind)>,
   776→        finally: Box<TypedExpr>,
   777→        finally_kind: PipelineAssignmentKind,
   778→    ) -> TypedExpr {
   779→        let first_value = self.pipeline_assignment(first_value);
   780→        let assignments = assignments
   781→            .into_iter()
   782→            .map(|(assignment, kind)| (self.pipeline_assignment(assignment), kind))
   783→            .collect();
   784→        let finally = self.boxed_expression(finally);
   785→
   786→        TypedExpr::Pipeline {
   787→            location,
   788→            first_value,
   789→            assignments,
   790→            finally,
   791→            finally_kind,
   792→        }
   793→    }
   794→
   795→    fn pipeline_assignment(
   796→        &mut self,
   797→        assignment: TypedPipelineAssignment,
   798→    ) -> TypedPipelineAssignment {
   799→        let TypedPipelineAssignment {
   800→            location,
   801→            name,
   802→            value,
   803→        } = assignment;
   804→
   805→        TypedPipelineAssignment {
   806→            location,
   807→            name,
   808→            value: self.boxed_expression(value),
   809→        }
   810→    }
   811→
   812→    fn bit_array(
   813→        &mut self,
   814→        location: SrcSpan,
   815→        type_: Arc<Type>,
   816→        segments: Vec<TypedExprBitArraySegment>,
   817→    ) -> TypedExpr {
   818→        let segments = segments
   819→            .into_iter()
   820→            .map(
   821→                |BitArraySegment {
   822→                     location,
   823→                     value,
   824→                     options,
   825→                     type_,
   826→                 }| BitArraySegment {
   827→                    location,
   828→                    value: self.boxed_expression(value),
   829→                    options: options
   830→                        .into_iter()
   831→                        .map(|bit_array_option| self.bit_array_option(bit_array_option))
   832→                        .collect(),
   833→                    type_,
   834→                },
   835→            )
   836→            .collect();
   837→
   838→        TypedExpr::BitArray {
   839→            location,
   840→            type_,
   841→            segments,
   842→        }
   843→    }
   844→
   845→    fn bit_array_option(&mut self, option: BitArrayOption<TypedExpr>) -> BitArrayOption<TypedExpr> {
   846→        match option {
   847→            BitArrayOption::Bytes { .. }
   848→            | BitArrayOption::Int { .. }
   849→            | BitArrayOption::Float { .. }
   850→            | BitArrayOption::Bits { .. }
   851→            | BitArrayOption::Utf8 { .. }
   852→            | BitArrayOption::Utf16 { .. }
   853→            | BitArrayOption::Utf32 { .. }
   854→            | BitArrayOption::Utf8Codepoint { .. }
   855→            | BitArrayOption::Utf16Codepoint { .. }
   856→            | BitArrayOption::Utf32Codepoint { .. }
   857→            | BitArrayOption::Signed { .. }
   858→            | BitArrayOption::Unsigned { .. }
   859→            | BitArrayOption::Big { .. }
   860→            | BitArrayOption::Little { .. }
   861→            | BitArrayOption::Native { .. }
   862→            | BitArrayOption::Unit { .. } => option,
   863→            BitArrayOption::Size {
   864→                location,
   865→                value,
   866→                short_form,
   867→            } => BitArrayOption::Size {
   868→                location,
   869→                value: self.boxed_expression(value),
   870→                short_form,
   871→            },
   872→        }
   873→    }
   874→
   875→    fn case(
   876→        &mut self,
   877→        location: SrcSpan,
   878→        type_: Arc<Type>,
   879→        subjects: Vec<TypedExpr>,
   880→        clauses: Vec<TypedClause>,
   881→        compiled_case: CompiledCase,
   882→    ) -> TypedExpr {
   883→        let subjects = self.expressions(subjects);
   884→        let clauses = clauses
   885→            .into_iter()
   886→            .map(
   887→                |TypedClause {
   888→                     location,
   889→                     pattern,
   890→                     alternative_patterns,
   891→                     guard,
   892→                     then,
   893→                 }| TypedClause {
   894→                    location,
   895→                    pattern,
   896→                    alternative_patterns,
   897→                    guard,
   898→                    then: self.expression(then),
   899→                },
   900→            )
   901→            .collect();
   902→
   903→        TypedExpr::Case {
   904→            location,
   905→            type_,
   906→            subjects,
   907→            clauses,
   908→            compiled_case,
   909→        }
   910→    }
   911→}
   912→
   913→fn find_inlinable_parameters(parameters: &[TypedArg], body: &[TypedStatement]) -> Vec<EcoString> {
   914→    let mut parameter_map = HashMap::new();
   915→    for parameter in parameters {
   916→        let (name, location) = match &parameter.names {
   917→            ArgNames::Discard { .. } | ArgNames::LabelledDiscard { .. } => continue,
   918→            ArgNames::Named { name, location }
   919→            | ArgNames::NamedLabelled {
   920→                name,
   921→                name_location: location,
   922→                ..
   923→            } => (name, location),
   924→        };
   925→        _ = parameter_map.insert((name.clone(), *location), false);
   926→    }
   927→
   928→    let mut finder = FindInlinableParameters {
   929→        parameters: parameter_map,
   930→        position: FunctionPosition::Body,
   931→    };
   932→    for statement in body {
   933→        finder.visit_typed_statement(statement);
   934→    }
   935→
   936→    // Inlinable parameters are those that are used exactly once. Any parameters
   937→    // used more than once will be removed from this map and so will not be
   938→    // considered inlinable.
   939→    finder
   940→        .parameters
   941→        .into_iter()
   942→        .filter_map(|((name, _), used)| used.then_some(name))
   943→        .collect()
   944→}
   945→
   946→/// A struct for finding the inlinable parameters of an anonymous function. Since
   947→/// we want to inline all anonymous functions, not just a subset of them like we
   948→/// do with regular functions, this must be implemented slightly differently, but
   949→/// it means we can take advantage of the AST visitor, since we don't need to
   950→/// transform the anonymous function into an intermediate representation.
   951→struct FindInlinableParameters {
   952→    parameters: HashMap<(EcoString, SrcSpan), bool>,
   953→    position: FunctionPosition,
   954→}
   955→
   956→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
   957→enum FunctionPosition {
   958→    Body,
   959→    NestedFunction,
   960→}
   961→
   962→impl FindInlinableParameters {
   963→    fn register_reference(&mut self, name: &EcoString, location: SrcSpan) {
   964→        let key = (name.clone(), location);
   965→
   966→        match self.position {
   967→            FunctionPosition::Body => {}
   968→            // We don't inline any parameters which are referenced in nested
   969→            // anonymous function, as our system for inlining parameters cannot
   970→            // properly handle that case; it requires more complex rewriting of
   971→            // the code in some cases.
   972→            FunctionPosition::NestedFunction => {
   973→                _ = self.parameters.remove(&key);
   974→                return;
   975→            }
   976→        }
   977→
   978→        match self.parameters.get_mut(&key) {
   979→            Some(true) => _ = self.parameters.remove(&key),
   980→            Some(used @ false) => {
   981→                *used = true;
   982→            }
   983→            None => {}
   984→        }
   985→    }
   986→}
   987→
   988→impl<'ast> Visit<'ast> for FindInlinableParameters {
   989→    fn visit_typed_expr_var(
   990→        &mut self,
   991→        _location: &'ast SrcSpan,
   992→        constructor: &'ast ValueConstructor,
   993→        name: &'ast EcoString,
   994→    ) {
   995→        if let ValueConstructorVariant::LocalVariable { location, .. } = constructor.variant {
   996→            self.register_reference(name, location);
   997→        }
   998→    }
   999→
  1000→    fn visit_typed_clause_guard_var(
  1001→        &mut self,
  1002→        _location: &'ast SrcSpan,
  1003→        name: &'ast EcoString,
  1004→        _type_: &'ast Arc<Type>,
  1005→        location: &'ast SrcSpan,
  1006→    ) {
  1007→        self.register_reference(name, *location);
  1008→    }
  1009→
  1010→    fn visit_typed_bit_array_size_variable(
  1011→        &mut self,
  1012→        _location: &'ast SrcSpan,
  1013→        name: &'ast EcoString,
  1014→        constructor: &'ast Option<Box<ValueConstructor>>,
  1015→        _type_: &'ast Arc<Type>,
  1016→    ) {
  1017→        let variant = match constructor {
  1018→            Some(constructor) => &constructor.variant,
  1019→            None => return,
  1020→        };
  1021→        if let ValueConstructorVariant::LocalVariable { location, .. } = variant {
  1022→            self.register_reference(name, *location);
  1023→        }
  1024→    }
  1025→
  1026→    fn visit_typed_expr_fn(
  1027→        &mut self,
  1028→        location: &'ast SrcSpan,
  1029→        type_: &'ast Arc<Type>,
  1030→        kind: &'ast FunctionLiteralKind,
  1031→        args: &'ast [TypedArg],
  1032→        body: &'ast Vec1<TypedStatement>,
  1033→        return_annotation: &'ast Option<ast::TypeAst>,
  1034→    ) {
  1035→        let previous_position = self.position;
  1036→        self.position = FunctionPosition::NestedFunction;
  1037→
  1038→        ast::visit::visit_typed_expr_fn(self, location, type_, kind, args, body, return_annotation);
  1039→
  1040→        self.position = previous_position;
  1041→    }
  1042→}
  1043→
  1044→/// Removes any blocks which are acting as brackets (they hold a single expression)
  1045→fn expand_block(expression: TypedExpr) -> TypedExpr {
  1046→    match expression {
  1047→        TypedExpr::Block {
  1048→            location,
  1049→            statements,
  1050→        } if statements.len() == 1 => {
  1051→            let (first, _rest) = statements.split_off_first();
  1052→
  1053→            match first {
  1054→                // If this is several blocks inside each other, we want to
  1055→                // expand them all.
  1056→                Statement::Expression(inner) => expand_block(inner),
  1057→                Statement::Assignment(_) | Statement::Use(_) | Statement::Assert(_) => {
  1058→                    TypedExpr::Block {
  1059→                        location,
  1060→                        statements: Vec1::new(first),
  1061→                    }
  1062→                }
  1063→            }
  1064→        }
  1065→        _ => expression,
  1066→    }
  1067→}
  1068→
  1069→/// Converts a function from the Gleam AST into a special "inlinable function",
  1070→/// which is a simplified version, containing just enough information for us to
  1071→/// perform inlining, while keeping the cache files to a minimum size.
  1072→///
  1073→/// This function also determines whether a function is inlinable. Currently this
  1074→/// just checks it against a list of stdlib functions we want to prioritise
  1075→/// inlining, but later it will be changed to a more complicated heuristic.
  1076→///
  1077→pub fn function_to_inlinable(
  1078→    package: &str,
  1079→    module: &str,
  1080→    function: &TypedFunction,
  1081→) -> Option<InlinableFunction> {
  1082→    let (_, name) = function.name.as_ref()?;
  1083→
  1084→    if !is_inlinable(package, module, name) {
  1085→        return None;
  1086→    }
  1087→
  1088→    let parameters = function
  1089→        .arguments
  1090→        .iter()
  1091→        .map(|argument| match &argument.names {
  1092→            ArgNames::Discard { name, .. } | ArgNames::Named { name, .. } => InlinableParameter {
  1093→                label: None,
  1094→                name: name.clone(),
  1095→            },
  1096→            ArgNames::LabelledDiscard { label, name, .. }
  1097→            | ArgNames::NamedLabelled { label, name, .. } => InlinableParameter {
  1098→                label: Some(label.clone()),
  1099→                name: name.clone(),
  1100→            },
  1101→        })
  1102→        .collect();
  1103→
  1104→    let mut converter = FunctionToInlinable::new(&function.arguments);
  1105→
  1106→    let body = function
  1107→        .body
  1108→        .iter()
  1109→        .map(|statement| converter.statement(statement))
  1110→        .collect::<Option<_>>()?;
  1111→
  1112→    // Figure out which parameters can be inlined within the body of this function.
  1113→    // When we inline a function, we convert it to a block with assignments for
  1114→    // the parameters. Then, if those parameters contain no side effects and are
  1115→    // only referenced once within the body, they can be inlined into the place
  1116→    // they are referenced.
  1117→    //
  1118→    // This code checks for the parameters which are used exactly once, which
  1119→    // can then be inlined. We can't inline parameters which are never used, as
  1120→    // there is nowhere to inline them to, so it doesn't make sense. We still
  1121→    // need to evaluate them though, as there could be side effects caused by
  1122→    // the values passed to them.
  1123→    let inlinable_parameters = converter
  1124→        .parameter_references
  1125→        .into_iter()
  1126→        .filter_map(|((name, _), used)| used.then_some(name))
  1127→        .collect();
  1128→
  1129→    Some(InlinableFunction {
  1130→        parameters,
  1131→        body,
  1132→        inlinable_parameters,
  1133→    })
  1134→}
  1135→
  1136→/// The heuristic to determine whether a function is inlinable. For now, this
  1137→/// just checks against a list of standard library functions.
  1138→fn is_inlinable(package: &str, module: &str, name: &str) -> bool {
  1139→    // For now we only offer inlining of standard library functions
  1140→    if package != STDLIB_PACKAGE_NAME {
  1141→        return false;
  1142→    }
  1143→
  1144→    match (module, name) {
  1145→        // These are the functions which we currently inline
  1146→        ("gleam/bool", "guard") => true,
  1147→        ("gleam/bool", "lazy_guard") => true,
  1148→        ("gleam/result", "try") => true,
  1149→        ("gleam/result", "map") => true,
  1150→        ("gleam/result", "map_error") => true,
  1151→        // For testing purposes it's useful to have a function which will always
  1152→        // be inlined, which we can define however we want. We only inline this
  1153→        // when we are in test mode though, because we wouldn't want this detail
  1154→        // leaking out into real-world code and causing unexpected behaviour.
  1155→        ("testing", "always_inline") => cfg!(test),
  1156→        _ => false,
  1157→    }
  1158→}
  1159→
  1160→/// Holds state for converting a `TypedFunction` into an `InlinableFunction`.
  1161→struct FunctionToInlinable {
  1162→    /// A map of parameters to a boolean of whether they have been used. Since
  1163→    /// Gleam has variable shadowing, we must also store the definition location
  1164→    /// of each parameter to ensure that it is not a variable shadowing the parameter
  1165→    /// name.
  1166→    /// If a parameter is used more than once, it is removed from the map, so it
  1167→    /// is no longer tracked as an inlinable parameter.
  1168→    parameter_references: HashMap<(EcoString, SrcSpan), bool>,
  1169→}
  1170→
  1171→impl FunctionToInlinable {
  1172→    fn new(arguments: &[TypedArg]) -> Self {
  1173→        let parameter_references = arguments
  1174→            .iter()
  1175→            .filter_map(|argument| {
  1176→                let (name, location) = match &argument.names {
  1177→                    ArgNames::Discard { .. } | ArgNames::LabelledDiscard { .. } => return None,
  1178→                    ArgNames::Named { name, location } => (name.clone(), *location),
  1179→                    ArgNames::NamedLabelled {
  1180→                        name,
  1181→                        name_location,
  1182→                        ..
  1183→                    } => (name.clone(), *name_location),
  1184→                };
  1185→
  1186→                Some(((name, location), false))
  1187→            })
  1188→            .collect();
  1189→
  1190→        Self {
  1191→            parameter_references,
  1192→        }
  1193→    }
  1194→
  1195→    fn statement(&mut self, statement: &TypedStatement) -> Option<InlinableExpression> {
  1196→        match statement {
  1197→            Statement::Expression(expression) => self.expression(expression),
  1198→            Statement::Assignment(_) | Statement::Use(_) | Statement::Assert(_) => None,
  1199→        }
  1200→    }
  1201→
  1202→    /// Converts an expression to an `InlinableExpression`. We only convert a
  1203→    /// small subset of the AST for now, enough to compile our desired inlinable
  1204→    /// stdlib functions. Anything else returns `None`, indicating a function
  1205→    /// cannot be inlined.
  1206→    fn expression(&mut self, expression: &TypedExpr) -> Option<InlinableExpression> {
  1207→        match expression {
  1208→            TypedExpr::Case {
  1209→                subjects,
  1210→                clauses,
  1211→                compiled_case,
  1212→                type_,
  1213→                ..
  1214→            } => {
  1215→                let subjects = subjects
  1216→                    .iter()
  1217→                    .map(|expression| self.expression(expression))
  1218→                    .collect::<Option<_>>()?;
  1219→                let clauses = clauses
  1220→                    .iter()
  1221→                    .map(|clause| self.clause(clause))
  1222→                    .collect::<Option<_>>()?;
  1223→
  1224→                Some(InlinableExpression::Case {
  1225→                    subjects,
  1226→                    clauses,
  1227→                    compiled_case: Box::new(compiled_case.clone()),
  1228→                    type_: self.type_(type_),
  1229→                })
  1230→            }
  1231→            TypedExpr::Var {
  1232→                constructor, name, ..
  1233→            } => {
  1234→                match &constructor.variant {
  1235→                    ValueConstructorVariant::LocalVariable { location, .. } => {
  1236→                        let key = (name.clone(), *location);
  1237→                        match self.parameter_references.get_mut(&key) {
  1238→                            Some(true) => {
  1239→                                _ = self.parameter_references.remove(&key);
  1240→                            }
  1241→                            Some(usage) => *usage = true,
  1242→                            None => {}
  1243→                        }
  1244→                    }
  1245→                    ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleConstant { .. }
  1246→                    | ValueConstructorVariant::LocalConstant { .. }
  1247→                    | ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleFn { .. }
  1248→                    | ValueConstructorVariant::Record { .. } => {}
  1249→                }
  1250→
  1251→                Some(InlinableExpression::Variable {
  1252→                    name: name.clone(),
  1253→                    constructor: self.value_constructor(constructor)?,
  1254→                    type_: self.type_(&constructor.type_),
  1255→                })
  1256→            }
  1257→            TypedExpr::Call {
  1258→                fun,
  1259→                arguments,
  1260→                type_,
  1261→                ..
  1262→            } => {
  1263→                let function = self.expression(fun)?;
  1264→                let arguments = arguments
  1265→                    .iter()
  1266→                    .map(|argument| {
  1267→                        Some(InlinableArgument {
  1268→                            label: argument.label.clone(),
  1269→                            value: self.expression(&argument.value)?,
  1270→                        })
  1271→                    })
  1272→                    .collect::<Option<_>>()?;
  1273→
  1274→                Some(InlinableExpression::Call {
  1275→                    function: Box::new(function),
  1276→                    arguments,
  1277→                    type_: self.type_(type_),
  1278→                })
  1279→            }
  1280→
  1281→            TypedExpr::Int { .. }
  1282→            | TypedExpr::Float { .. }
  1283→            | TypedExpr::String { .. }
  1284→            | TypedExpr::Block { .. }
  1285→            | TypedExpr::Pipeline { .. }
  1286→            | TypedExpr::Fn { .. }
  1287→            | TypedExpr::List { .. }
  1288→            | TypedExpr::BinOp { .. }
  1289→            | TypedExpr::RecordAccess { .. }
  1290→            | TypedExpr::ModuleSelect { .. }
  1291→            | TypedExpr::Tuple { .. }
  1292→            | TypedExpr::TupleIndex { .. }
  1293→            | TypedExpr::Todo { .. }
  1294→            | TypedExpr::Panic { .. }
  1295→            | TypedExpr::Echo { .. }
  1296→            | TypedExpr::BitArray { .. }
  1297→            | TypedExpr::RecordUpdate { .. }
  1298→            | TypedExpr::NegateBool { .. }
  1299→            | TypedExpr::NegateInt { .. }
  1300→            | TypedExpr::Invalid { .. } => None,
  1301→        }
  1302→    }
  1303→
  1304→    fn type_(&self, type_: &Arc<Type>) -> InlinableType {
  1305→        match collapse_links(type_.clone()).as_ref() {
  1306→            Type::Fn { arguments, return_ } => InlinableType::Function {
  1307→                arguments: arguments
  1308→                    .iter()
  1309→                    .map(|argument| self.type_(argument))
  1310→                    .collect(),
  1311→                return_: Box::new(self.type_(return_)),
  1312→            },
  1313→            Type::Named {
  1314→                module,
  1315→                name,
  1316→                arguments,
  1317→                ..
  1318→            } if module == PRELUDE_MODULE_NAME => self.prelude_type(name, arguments),
  1319→            Type::Named { .. } | Type::Var { .. } | Type::Tuple { .. } => InlinableType::Other,
  1320→        }
  1321→    }
  1322→
  1323→    fn prelude_type(&self, name: &str, arguments: &[Arc<Type>]) -> InlinableType {
  1324→        match (name, arguments) {
  1325→            ("BitArray", _) => InlinableType::BitArray,
  1326→            ("Bool", _) => InlinableType::Bool,
  1327→            ("Float", _) => InlinableType::Float,
  1328→            ("Int", _) => InlinableType::Int,
  1329→            ("List", [element]) => InlinableType::List(Box::new(self.type_(element))),
  1330→            ("Nil", _) => InlinableType::Nil,
  1331→            ("Result", [ok, error]) => InlinableType::Result {
  1332→                ok: Box::new(self.type_(ok)),
  1333→                error: Box::new(self.type_(error)),
  1334→            },
  1335→            ("String", _) => InlinableType::String,
  1336→            ("UtfCodepoint", _) => InlinableType::UtfCodepoint,
  1337→            _ => InlinableType::Other,
  1338→        }
  1339→    }
  1340→
  1341→    fn value_constructor(
  1342→        &mut self,
  1343→        constructor: &ValueConstructor,
  1344→    ) -> Option<InlinableValueConstructor> {
  1345→        match &constructor.variant {
  1346→            ValueConstructorVariant::LocalVariable { .. } => {
  1347→                Some(InlinableValueConstructor::LocalVariable)
  1348→            }
  1349→            ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleConstant { .. }
  1350→            | ValueConstructorVariant::LocalConstant { .. } => None,
  1351→            ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleFn { name, module, .. } => {
  1352→                Some(InlinableValueConstructor::Function {
  1353→                    name: name.clone(),
  1354→                    module: module.clone(),
  1355→                })
  1356→            }
  1357→            ValueConstructorVariant::Record { name, module, .. } => {
  1358→                Some(InlinableValueConstructor::Record {
  1359→                    name: name.clone(),
  1360→                    module: module.clone(),
  1361→                })
  1362→            }
  1363→        }
  1364→    }
  1365→
  1366→    fn clause(&mut self, clause: &TypedClause) -> Option<InlinableClause> {
  1367→        let pattern = clause
  1368→            .pattern
  1369→            .iter()
  1370→            .map(Self::pattern)
  1371→            .collect::<Option<_>>()?;
  1372→        let body = self.expression(&clause.then)?;
  1373→        Some(InlinableClause { pattern, body })
  1374→    }
  1375→
  1376→    fn pattern(pattern: &TypedPattern) -> Option<InlinablePattern> {
  1377→        match pattern {
  1378→            TypedPattern::Variable { name, .. } => {
  1379→                Some(InlinablePattern::Variable { name: name.clone() })
  1380→            }
  1381→            TypedPattern::Constructor {
  1382→                name,
  1383→                arguments,
  1384→                constructor: Inferred::Known(inferred),
  1385→                ..
  1386→            } => {
  1387→                let arguments = arguments
  1388→                    .iter()
  1389→                    .map(|argument| {
  1390→                        Some(InlinableArgument {
  1391→                            label: argument.label.clone(),
  1392→                            value: Self::pattern(&argument.value)?,
  1393→                        })
  1394→                    })
  1395→                    .collect::<Option<_>>()?;
  1396→
  1397→                Some(InlinablePattern::Constructor {
  1398→                    name: name.clone(),
  1399→                    module: inferred.module.clone(),
  1400→                    arguments,
  1401→                })
  1402→            }
  1403→
  1404→            TypedPattern::Constructor {
  1405→                constructor: Inferred::Unknown,
  1406→                ..
  1407→            } => None,
  1408→
  1409→            TypedPattern::Int { .. }
  1410→            | TypedPattern::Float { .. }
  1411→            | TypedPattern::String { .. }
  1412→            | TypedPattern::BitArraySize { .. }
  1413→            | TypedPattern::Assign { .. }
  1414→            | TypedPattern::Discard { .. }
  1415→            | TypedPattern::List { .. }
  1416→            | TypedPattern::Tuple { .. }
  1417→            | TypedPattern::BitArray { .. }
  1418→            | TypedPattern::StringPrefix { .. }
  1419→            | TypedPattern::Invalid { .. } => None,
  1420→        }
  1421→    }
  1422→}
  1423→
  1424→/// A simplified version of a `TypedFunction`.
  1425→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1426→pub struct InlinableFunction {
  1427→    pub parameters: Vec<InlinableParameter>,
  1428→    pub body: Vec<InlinableExpression>,
  1429→    /// A list of parameters which are only referenced once and can therefore
  1430→    /// be inlined within the body of this function.
  1431→    pub inlinable_parameters: Vec<EcoString>,
  1432→}
  1433→
  1434→/// Location information is not stored for inlinable functions, to reduce cache
  1435→/// size. The only reason we should need location information is for generating
  1436→/// code for panicking keywords, like `panic` or `todo`.
  1437→///
  1438→/// Those are not supported yet, and when they are they will likely require some
  1439→/// more thought as to how they are implemented, as inlining a function completely
  1440→/// changes its location in the codebase.
  1441→const BLANK_LOCATION: SrcSpan = SrcSpan { start: 0, end: 0 };
  1442→
  1443→impl InlinableFunction {
  1444→    /// Converts an `InlinableFunction` to an anonymous function, which can then
  1445→    /// be inlined within another function.
  1446→    fn to_anonymous_function(&self) -> (Vec<TypedArg>, Vec1<TypedStatement>) {
  1447→        let parameters = self
  1448→            .parameters
  1449→            .iter()
  1450→            .map(|parameter| parameter.to_typed_arg())
  1451→            .collect();
  1452→
  1453→        let body = self
  1454→            .body
  1455→            .iter()
  1456→            .map(|ast| Statement::Expression(ast.to_expression()))
  1457→            .collect_vec();
  1458→
  1459→        (
  1460→            parameters,
  1461→            body.try_into()
  1462→                .expect("Type-checking ensured that the body has at least 1 statement"),
  1463→        )
  1464→    }
  1465→}
  1466→
  1467→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1468→pub enum InlinableExpression {
  1469→    Case {
  1470→        subjects: Vec<InlinableExpression>,
  1471→        clauses: Vec<InlinableClause>,
  1472→        compiled_case: Box<CompiledCase>,
  1473→        type_: InlinableType,
  1474→    },
  1475→
  1476→    Variable {
  1477→        name: EcoString,
  1478→        constructor: InlinableValueConstructor,
  1479→        type_: InlinableType,
  1480→    },
  1481→
  1482→    Call {
  1483→        function: Box<InlinableExpression>,
  1484→        arguments: Vec<InlinableArgument<InlinableExpression>>,
  1485→        type_: InlinableType,
  1486→    },
  1487→}
  1488→
  1489→impl InlinableExpression {
  1490→    fn to_expression(&self) -> TypedExpr {
  1491→        match self {
  1492→            InlinableExpression::Case {
  1493→                subjects,
  1494→                clauses,
  1495→                compiled_case,
  1496→                type_,
  1497→            } => TypedExpr::Case {
  1498→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1499→                type_: type_.to_type(),
  1500→                subjects: subjects
  1501→                    .iter()
  1502→                    .map(|subject| subject.to_expression())
  1503→                    .collect(),
  1504→                clauses: clauses
  1505→                    .iter()
  1506→                    .map(|clause| clause.to_typed_clause())
  1507→                    .collect(),
  1508→                compiled_case: compiled_case.as_ref().clone(),
  1509→            },
  1510→            InlinableExpression::Variable {
  1511→                name,
  1512→                constructor,
  1513→                type_,
  1514→            } => TypedExpr::Var {
  1515→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1516→                constructor: constructor.to_value_constructor(type_.to_type()),
  1517→                name: name.clone(),
  1518→            },
  1519→            InlinableExpression::Call {
  1520→                function,
  1521→                arguments,
  1522→                type_,
  1523→            } => TypedExpr::Call {
  1524→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1525→                type_: type_.to_type(),
  1526→                fun: Box::new(function.to_expression()),
  1527→                arguments: arguments
  1528→                    .iter()
  1529→                    .map(|argument| argument.to_call_arg(Self::to_expression))
  1530→                    .collect(),
  1531→            },
  1532→        }
  1533→    }
  1534→}
  1535→
  1536→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1537→pub struct InlinableClause {
  1538→    pub pattern: Vec<InlinablePattern>,
  1539→    pub body: InlinableExpression,
  1540→}
  1541→
  1542→impl InlinableClause {
  1543→    fn to_typed_clause(&self) -> TypedClause {
  1544→        TypedClause {
  1545→            location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1546→            pattern: self
  1547→                .pattern
  1548→                .iter()
  1549→                .map(|pattern| pattern.to_typed_pattern())
  1550→                .collect(),
  1551→            alternative_patterns: Vec::new(),
  1552→            guard: None,
  1553→            then: self.body.to_expression(),
  1554→        }
  1555→    }
  1556→}
  1557→
  1558→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1559→pub enum InlinablePattern {
  1560→    Constructor {
  1561→        name: EcoString,
  1562→        module: EcoString,
  1563→        arguments: Vec<InlinableArgument<InlinablePattern>>,
  1564→    },
  1565→
  1566→    Variable {
  1567→        name: EcoString,
  1568→    },
  1569→}
  1570→
  1571→impl InlinablePattern {
  1572→    fn to_typed_pattern(&self) -> TypedPattern {
  1573→        match self {
  1574→            InlinablePattern::Constructor {
  1575→                name,
  1576→                module,
  1577→                arguments,
  1578→            } => TypedPattern::Constructor {
  1579→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1580→                name_location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1581→                name: name.clone(),
  1582→                arguments: arguments
  1583→                    .iter()
  1584→                    .map(|argument| argument.to_call_arg(Self::to_typed_pattern))
  1585→                    .collect(),
  1586→                module: None,
  1587→                constructor: Inferred::Known(PatternConstructor {
  1588→                    name: name.clone(),
  1589→                    field_map: None,
  1590→                    documentation: None,
  1591→                    module: module.clone(),
  1592→                    location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1593→                    constructor_index: 0,
  1594→                }),
  1595→                spread: None,
  1596→                type_: unknown_type(),
  1597→            },
  1598→            InlinablePattern::Variable { name } => TypedPattern::Variable {
  1599→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1600→                name: name.clone(),
  1601→                type_: unknown_type(),
  1602→                origin: VariableOrigin::generated(),
  1603→            },
  1604→        }
  1605→    }
  1606→}
  1607→
  1608→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1609→pub enum InlinableValueConstructor {
  1610→    LocalVariable,
  1611→    Function { name: EcoString, module: EcoString },
  1612→    Record { name: EcoString, module: EcoString },
  1613→}
  1614→
  1615→impl InlinableValueConstructor {
  1616→    fn to_value_constructor(&self, type_: Arc<Type>) -> ValueConstructor {
  1617→        let variant = match self {
  1618→            InlinableValueConstructor::LocalVariable => ValueConstructorVariant::LocalVariable {
  1619→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1620→                origin: VariableOrigin::generated(),
  1621→            },
  1622→            InlinableValueConstructor::Function { name, module } => {
  1623→                ValueConstructorVariant::ModuleFn {
  1624→                    name: name.clone(),
  1625→                    field_map: None,
  1626→                    module: module.clone(),
  1627→                    arity: 0,
  1628→                    location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1629→                    documentation: None,
  1630→                    implementations: Implementations::supporting_all(),
  1631→                    external_erlang: None,
  1632→                    external_javascript: None,
  1633→                    purity: Purity::Unknown,
  1634→                }
  1635→            }
  1636→            InlinableValueConstructor::Record { name, module } => ValueConstructorVariant::Record {
  1637→                name: name.clone(),
  1638→                arity: 0,
  1639→                field_map: None,
  1640→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1641→                module: module.clone(),
  1642→                variants_count: 0,
  1643→                variant_index: 0,
  1644→                documentation: None,
  1645→            },
  1646→        };
  1647→        ValueConstructor {
  1648→            publicity: Publicity::Private,
  1649→            deprecation: Deprecation::NotDeprecated,
  1650→            variant,
  1651→            type_,
  1652→        }
  1653→    }
  1654→}
  1655→
  1656→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1657→pub struct InlinableArgument<T> {
  1658→    pub label: Option<EcoString>,
  1659→    pub value: T,
  1660→}
  1661→
  1662→impl<T> InlinableArgument<T> {
  1663→    fn to_call_arg<U, F>(&self, convert_value: F) -> CallArg<U>
  1664→    where
  1665→        F: FnOnce(&T) -> U,
  1666→    {
  1667→        CallArg {
  1668→            label: self.label.clone(),
  1669→            location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1670→            value: convert_value(&self.value),
  1671→            implicit: None,
  1672→        }
  1673→    }
  1674→}
  1675→
  1676→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1677→pub struct InlinableParameter {
  1678→    pub label: Option<EcoString>,
  1679→    pub name: EcoString,
  1680→}
  1681→
  1682→impl InlinableParameter {
  1683→    fn to_typed_arg(&self) -> TypedArg {
  1684→        let is_discard = self.name.starts_with('_');
  1685→
  1686→        let names = match &self.label {
  1687→            Some(label) if is_discard => ArgNames::LabelledDiscard {
  1688→                label: label.clone(),
  1689→                label_location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1690→                name: self.name.clone(),
  1691→                name_location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1692→            },
  1693→            Some(label) => ArgNames::NamedLabelled {
  1694→                label: label.clone(),
  1695→                label_location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1696→                name: self.name.clone(),
  1697→                name_location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1698→            },
  1699→            None if is_discard => ArgNames::Discard {
  1700→                name: self.name.clone(),
  1701→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1702→            },
  1703→            None => ArgNames::Named {
  1704→                name: self.name.clone(),
  1705→                location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1706→            },
  1707→        };
  1708→
  1709→        TypedArg {
  1710→            names,
  1711→            location: BLANK_LOCATION,
  1712→            annotation: None,
  1713→            type_: unknown_type(),
  1714→        }
  1715→    }
  1716→}
  1717→
  1718→/// A simplified version of `Type`, which only cares about prelude types. Code
  1719→/// generation needs this type information, as some prelude types are handled
  1720→/// specially in certain cases. Custom type don't matter though, so they all get
  1721→/// reduced into a single value, which decreases cache size.
  1722→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize)]
  1723→pub enum InlinableType {
  1724→    BitArray,
  1725→    Bool,
  1726→    Float,
  1727→    Int,
  1728→    List(Box<InlinableType>),
  1729→    Nil,
  1730→    Result {
  1731→        ok: Box<InlinableType>,
  1732→        error: Box<InlinableType>,
  1733→    },
  1734→    String,
  1735→    UtfCodepoint,
  1736→
  1737→    Function {
  1738→        arguments: Vec<InlinableType>,
  1739→        return_: Box<InlinableType>,
  1740→    },
  1741→
  1742→    Other,
  1743→}
  1744→
  1745→fn unknown_type() -> Arc<Type> {
  1746→    type_::generic_var(0)
  1747→}
  1748→
  1749→impl InlinableType {
  1750→    fn to_type(&self) -> Arc<Type> {
  1751→        match self {
  1752→            InlinableType::BitArray => type_::bit_array(),
  1753→            InlinableType::Bool => type_::bool(),
  1754→            InlinableType::Float => type_::float(),
  1755→            InlinableType::Int => type_::int(),
  1756→            InlinableType::List(element) => type_::list(element.to_type()),
  1757→            InlinableType::Nil => type_::nil(),
  1758→            InlinableType::Result { ok, error } => type_::result(ok.to_type(), error.to_type()),
  1759→            InlinableType::String => type_::string(),
  1760→            InlinableType::UtfCodepoint => type_::utf_codepoint(),
  1761→
  1762→            InlinableType::Function { arguments, return_ } => type_::fn_(
  1763→                arguments.iter().map(Self::to_type).collect(),
  1764→                return_.to_type(),
  1765→            ),
  1766→
  1767→            // Code generation doesn't care about custom types at all, only
  1768→            // prelude types are handled specially, so we treat custom types as
  1769→            // opaque generic type variables.
  1770→            InlinableType::Other => unknown_type(),
  1771→        }
  1772→    }
  1773→}

commit a820a0ac5e6a65573a7fd68216efe3bdf8434561
Author: Gears <gearsOfficial@proton.me>

    Disable inlining

diff --git a/CHANGELOG.md b/CHANGELOG.md
index a3c1834dd..e9d765f62 100644
--- a/CHANGELOG.md
+++ b/CHANGELOG.md
@@ -4,38 +4,6 @@
 
 ### Compiler
 
-- The compiler now performs function inlining optimisations for a specific set
-  of standard library functions, which can allow functions which were previously
-  not tail-recursive on the JavaScript target to become tail-recursive. For
-  example, the following code:
-
-  ```gleam
-  pub fn count(from: Int, to: Int) -> Int {
-    use <- bool.guard(when: from >= to, return: from)
-    io.println(int.to_string())
-    count(from + 1, to)
-  }
-  ```
-
-  Would previously cause a stack overflow on the JavaScript target for large
-  values. Now it is rewritten to:
-
-  ```gleam
-  pub fn count(from: Int, to: Int) -> Int {
-    case from >= to {
-      True -> from
-      False -> {
-        io.println(int.to_string())
-        count(from + 1, to)
-      }
-    }
-  }
-  ```
-
-  Which allows tail-call optimisation to occur.
-
-  ([Surya Rose](https://github.com/GearsDatapacks))
-
 - The compiler now applies an optimisation known as "interference based pruning"
   when compiling bit array pattern matching where matches are performed at the
   start of bit arrays.

index 778d105d3..1ebe8f557 100644
--- a/compiler-core/src/build/package_compiler.rs
+++ b/compiler-core/src/build/package_compiler.rs
@@ -214,17 +214,20 @@ where
 
         tracing::debug!("performing_code_generation");

-            modules
-                .into_iter()
-                .map(|mut module| {
-                    module.ast = inline::module(module.ast, &existing_modules);
-                    module
-                })
-                .collect()

 ### Compiler
 
-- The compiler now performs function inlining optimisations for a specific set
-  of standard library functions, which can allow functions which were previously
-  not tail-recursive on the JavaScript target to become tail-recursive. For
-  example, the following code:
-
-  ```gleam
-  pub fn count(from: Int, to: Int) -> Int {
-    use <- bool.guard(when: from >= to, return: from)
-    io.println(int.to_string())
-    count(from + 1, to)
-  }
-  ```
-
-  Would previously cause a stack overflow on the JavaScript target for large
-  values. Now it is rewritten to:
-
-  ```gleam
-  pub fn count(from: Int, to: Int) -> Int {
-    case from >= to {
-      True -> from
-      False -> {
-        io.println(int.to_string())
-        count(from + 1, to)
-      }
-    }
-  }
-  ```
-
-  Which allows tail-call optimisation to occur.
-
